title: Two men, no names
summary: Two strangers share a hot wooden bench in a public sauna in Helsinki. They never ask each other's names.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Your shoulders are up. After a while they will come down.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Eighty-five degrees. A normal afternoon. The top bench is hotter. Stay on the middle one for now.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: Thank you. I am from Madrid. I am here for one week. My wife is at the lake outside. She did not want to come in.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Many people do not. The body needs time to learn.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: Can I ask — do you come here often?

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Three times a week for thirty years. Same bench. Same time.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Mostly. We do not ask each other's names. We know each other by our backs, our coughs, our small habits. A man here lost his wife last year. We knew because he sat lower for two months.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: We sat closer. That is speaking, here.

body.speaker: Visitor
body.text: In Madrid we would have asked him about it.

body.speaker: Older man
body.text: Maybe that is good too. We do it our way. In the heat, words come slowly. The body says more.

annotations.meaningVi: sau một lúc / một thời gian
băng ghế trên cùng (trong phòng tắm hơi)
cái ở giữa (ở đây là băng ghế giữa)
qua/dựa vào lưng của chúng tôi
ngồi thấp hơn — past simple của 'sit' = 'sat'
trong cái nóng / khi trời nóng
những từ đến chậm / khó nói nên lời
tôi để ý / nhận ra rồi — 'notice' = nhận thấy

grammarNotes.title: Past simple of 'sit', 'lose', 'know'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Một số động từ thường gặp có quá khứ bất quy tắc. Trong bài: 'sat' (sit → sat), 'lost' (lose → lost), 'knew' (know → knew), 'said' (say → said). Câu mẫu: 'A man here lost his wife last year. We knew because he sat lower.' Hãy học theo bộ ba V1-V2-V3: sit-sat-sat; lose-lost-lost; know-knew-known; say-said-said.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Several common verbs have irregular past forms. From the dialogue: 'sat' (sit → sat), 'lost' (lose → lost), 'knew' (know → knew), 'said' (say → said). Example: 'A man here lost his wife last year. We knew because he sat lower.' Learn them as V1-V2-V3 sets: sit-sat-sat; lose-lost-lost; know-knew-known; say-said-said.

translationVi:
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Lần đầu hả?
Du khách: Có rõ thế không?
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Vai anh đang đưa lên cao. Một lúc nữa nó sẽ hạ xuống.
Du khách: Nóng quá.
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Tám mươi lăm độ. Một buổi chiều bình thường. Băng trên cùng còn nóng hơn. Tạm ngồi băng giữa thôi.
Du khách: Cảm ơn. Tôi từ Madrid. Tôi đến đây một tuần. Vợ tôi đang ở hồ ngoài kia. Cô ấy không muốn vào.
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Nhiều người không vào. Cơ thể cần thời gian để quen.
Du khách: Tôi hỏi nhé — ông có hay đến đây không?
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Ba lần một tuần, suốt ba mươi năm. Cùng một băng ghế. Cùng giờ.
Du khách: Cùng những người à?
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Phần lớn vậy. Chúng tôi không hỏi tên nhau. Chúng tôi nhận ra nhau qua lưng, qua tiếng ho, qua những thói quen nhỏ. Có một ông ở đây mất vợ năm ngoái. Chúng tôi biết vì ông ấy ngồi thấp hơn suốt hai tháng.
Du khách: Có ai nói chuyện với ông ấy không?
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Chúng tôi ngồi gần hơn. Đó là cách nói chuyện, ở đây.
Du khách: Ở Madrid chúng tôi sẽ hỏi ông ấy về chuyện đó.
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Cách đó cũng có thể tốt. Còn ở đây chúng tôi làm theo cách của mình. Trong cái nóng, lời nói đến chậm. Cơ thể nói nhiều hơn.
Du khách: Vai tôi hạ xuống rồi.
Người đàn ông lớn tuổi: Tôi để ý. Chào mừng anh.

questions.prompt: How does the older man know the visitor is new?
options: He is asking too many questions

questions.prompt: Why should the visitor stay on the middle bench?
options: It is closer to the door
questions.explanation: 'The top bench is hotter. Stay on the middle one for now.'

questions.prompt: Where is the visitor from, and where is his wife?
options: He is from Madrid; she is at the lake outside
He is from Helsinki; she is at home
He is from Paris; she is in the sauna
He is from Madrid; she is also in the sauna

questions.prompt: How often and how long has the older man come?
options: Three times a week for thirty years

questions.prompt: How do the regulars recognise each other?
options: By backs, coughs, and small habits — they do not ask names
questions.explanation: 'We know each other by our backs, our coughs, our small habits.'

questions.prompt: How did they know one man had lost his wife?
options: He sat lower for two months

questions.prompt: How did they respond to him?
options: They sat closer — 'that is speaking, here'

questions.prompt: What contrast does the visitor draw with Madrid?
options: In Madrid they would have asked him about it
In Madrid they would have ignored him
In Madrid no one goes to saunas
questions.explanation: 'In Madrid we would have asked him about it.'

questions.prompt: Does the older man dismiss Madrid's way?
options: No — 'maybe that is good too' but they do it their way
Two cultures handle grief differently, and both can be respectful in their own form.
questions.explanation: The dialogue is a small cross-cultural meeting.
questions.hint: Notice the openness of the older man.

cloze.template:
Older man: First time?
Visitor: Is it {{b1}}?
Older man: Your shoulders are up. After a while they will come down.
Visitor: It is very hot.
Older man: Eighty-five {{b2}}. A normal afternoon. The top bench is hotter. Stay on the middle one for now.
Visitor: Thank you. I am from Madrid. I am here for one week. My wife is at the lake outside. She did not want to come in.
Older man: Many people do not. The body needs time to {{b3}}.
Visitor: Can I ask — do you come here often?
Older man: Three times a week for thirty years. Same {{b4}}. Same time.
Visitor: Same people?
Older man: Mostly. We do not ask each other's {{b5}}. We know each other by our backs, our coughs, our small habits. A man here lost his wife last year. We {{b6}} because he sat lower for two months.
Visitor: Did anyone speak to him?
Older man: We sat {{b7}}. That is speaking, here.
Visitor: In Madrid we would have asked him about it.
Older man: Maybe that is good too. We do it our way. In the heat, words come {{b8}}. The body says more.
Visitor: My shoulders are {{b9}}.
Older man: I noticed. {{b10}}.

blanks.explanation: The body needs time to learn.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The older man says, 'We sat closer. That is speaking, here.' Think of a time someone showed they cared without using words. What did they do, and would words have made it stronger or weaker?
